selection_criteria: Retain CVEs from all_cves.json only when they are not fundamentally memory-corruption issues and could still affect a Rust reimplementation of liblzma. Exclude C-specific memory-safety bugs, command-line or script-only issues outside liblzma, and disputed or bogus reports.

relevant_cves.summary: XZ Utils 5.6.0 and 5.6.1 release tarballs contained malicious code that altered liblzma during the build.
relevant_cves.why_relevant_to_rust_reimplementation: This issue came from the release and build pipeline, not from C memory unsafety. A Rust port would still be vulnerable if its crate releases, source tarballs, build scripts, CI artifacts, or signing workflow were compromised.
rust_reimplementation_considerations: Publish from auditable version-controlled source and verify that release artifacts match the tagged source.
Keep release builds free of opaque prebuilt objects, disguised test assets, or other inputs that can silently alter the shipped library.
Minimize build-time code execution and treat build scripts, code generators, and packaging steps as part of the security boundary.
Use reproducible builds and strict maintainer controls for release signing and publishing.

excluded_after_analysis.reason: Excluded because the reported impact is a threaded-decoder race that manifests as use-after-free and null-based invalid writes. That is primarily a memory-safety bug class that a safe Rust ownership model should eliminate, even though equivalent decoder-state tests are still worth keeping.
Excluded because it is a heap buffer overflow caused by an undersized allocation after empty-Index decoding. This is a memory-corruption issue that a safe Rust port should not reproduce directly.
Excluded because it is a non-memory-corruption issue in native Windows command-line tools; the official advisory states that liblzma is unaffected.
Excluded because it affects xzgrep filename handling rather than the liblzma library.
Excluded because it affects the xzgrep script rather than the liblzma library.
Excluded because the upstream project documents it as a bogus CVE and not a real liblzma security issue.
